topic: Indian National Congress & The Moderate Phase (1885–1905)

questions.text: The Indian National Congress was founded in 1885. Who was its founder, and who presided over the first session?
options: Founded by Dadabhai Naoroji; first president W.C. Bonnerjee
Founded by A.O. Hume; first president W.C. Bonnerjee
Founded by A.O. Hume; first president Dadabhai Naoroji
Founded by Surendranath Banerjee; first president Pherozeshah Mehta
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress was founded by Allan Octavian Hume (A.O. Hume), a retired British ICS officer, on 28 December 1885, at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College, Bombay. The first session was presided over by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee (W.C. Bonnerji), a Calcutta barrister. Hume organised the Congress and remained its driving force, but did not preside over its sessions. Dadabhai Naoroji presided over the second session (1886, Calcutta). Surendranath Banerjee had founded the Indian Association (1876) and organised two National Conferences (1883, 1885) — important precursors to the INC but not the INC itself.
questions.source: SSC CGL — INC founder (A.O. Hume) and first president (W.C. Bonnerjee) is one of the most consistently tested founding facts

questions.text: Who was the first Muslim President of the Indian National Congress?
options: Sir Syed Ahmad Khan
Badruddin Tyabji
Aga Khan III
Mohammad Ali Jinnah
questions.explanation: Badruddin Tyabji was the first Muslim President of the Indian National Congress, presiding over the third session in Madras in 1887. This is historically significant because Sir Syed Ahmad Khan — another prominent Muslim leader of the same era — actively discouraged Muslims from joining the Congress. Tyabji's presidency demonstrated that the early Congress was multi-religious and that not all Muslim leaders agreed with Sir Syed's separatist position. Mohammad Ali Jinnah joined Congress later and also served as president (1916 Lucknow session) — but Tyabji was first. The Aga Khan led the Muslim delegation to Viceroy Minto in 1906, which led to separate electorates under Morley-Minto Reforms.
questions.source: SSC CGL — first Muslim INC President (Badruddin Tyabji, 1887) is a standard 'first' question; commonly confused with Sir Syed (who opposed Congress) or Jinnah

questions.text: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded the Servants of India Society in 1905. Gandhi called Gokhale his 'political guru.' What was the Servants of India Society?
options: A secret revolutionary organisation that planned armed resistance against British rule
An organisation that trained Indians for professional public service — members pledged lifelong service to the nation, living simply and researching social and political conditions
A welfare body that provided free legal aid to Indian factory workers in British-owned mills
The political wing of the Arya Samaj, dedicated to promoting Hindi as the national language
questions.explanation: The Servants of India Society (1905), founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale, was a non-political organisation dedicated to training Indians as professional social workers and reformers. Members took vows of poverty and lifelong service — they received only basic maintenance stipends and dedicated themselves entirely to public work: researching poverty, representing workers, educating communities. It was modelled partly on Christian missionary orders but secular and nationalist in purpose. Gandhi considered Gokhale his political guru because Gokhale's patient, research-based approach — engaging deeply with the system through the Legislative Council and systematic economic arguments — shaped Gandhi's early political philosophy. Gokhale told Gandhi to spend a year travelling India before taking political positions; Gandhi followed this advice.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Servants of India Society (Gokhale, 1905) and its purpose is a standard identification question

questions.text: The 'Safety Valve Theory' regarding the founding of the Indian National Congress suggests that it was created to serve British interests. Who first used this term critically?
options: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
Lala Lajpat Rai
Bipin Chandra Pal
Aurobindo Ghosh
questions.explanation: The 'Safety Valve Theory' was first articulated critically by Lala Lajpat Rai. The theory argues that A.O. Hume and sympathetic British officials (including possibly Viceroy Lord Dufferin) designed the Indian National Congress as a 'safety valve' — a constitutional outlet for educated Indian discontent, channeling it harmlessly into resolutions and petitions rather than allowing it to build into revolutionary action. Lajpat Rai used this term to question the Congress's legitimacy and to argue for more militant nationalism. Whether accurate or not (Hume's own letters suggest genuine concern for Indian welfare), the theory became influential in explaining why the Extremists mistrusted the Moderate approach.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the 'Safety Valve Theory' and Lala Lajpat Rai as the person who coined/used it is a standard attribution question in INC founding questions

questions.text: The Indian National Congress was supposed to hold its first session in Pune but it was shifted to Bombay. Why?
options: The Pune authorities refused permission because they feared the British government's reaction
A cholera/plague outbreak in Pune made the city unsuitable; the venue was shifted to Bombay
Bal Gangadhar Tilak opposed holding the Congress in Pune and threatened to disrupt the session
A.O. Hume preferred Bombay because it had better railway connections for delegates from all parts of India
questions.explanation: The first Indian National Congress session was originally planned for Pune, but an outbreak of cholera in the city made it impractical to hold a large gathering there. The venue was shifted to Bombay (Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College), which is why the INC's founding session is associated with Bombay. This is a detail that often surprises students — the planned city (Pune) vs. the actual city (Bombay) is a specific trap in questions phrased as 'the first session was planned to be held at...' or 'why was the first session held in Bombay?'
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the Pune-to-Bombay shift is a specific detail-level question that appears in higher-difficulty papers; students know Bombay but not the reason for the shift

questions.text: Dadabhai Naoroji presided over the INC three times — including the crucial 1906 Calcutta session. What made the 1906 session historically significant?
options: It was the first session to pass a resolution demanding complete independence (Purna Swaraj) from British rule
Naoroji introduced 'Swaraj' (self-rule) as the Congress's official goal — the first time Congress had explicitly demanded self-government rather than just better governance under British rule
The session resulted in the Surat Split between Moderates and Extremists
Naoroji introduced the resolution that founded the Muslim League as a separate party
questions.explanation: The 1906 Calcutta session of INC, presided over by Dadabhai Naoroji, was historic because Naoroji introduced 'Swaraj' (self-rule/self-government) as the official goal of the Indian National Congress. Before this, Congress resolutions had talked about 'good government' and 'reforms' within the British system — not self-government. The 1906 session was a significant shift: Congress was now explicitly saying it wanted Indians to govern themselves. Naoroji, the Grand Old Man respected by both Moderates and Extremists, was specifically chosen to preside because his stature might bridge the widening divide. The Surat Split happened at the NEXT year's session (1907 Surat) — it's a common confusion. 'Purna Swaraj' (complete independence) came much later — 1929 Lahore session under Nehru.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the 1906 session's significance (Swaraj as Congress goal) is often confused with Surat Split (1907) or Purna Swaraj (1929); this three-way distinction is tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The Indian Councils Act of 1892 was the main constitutional reform won by the Moderate Congress. Why did Extremists consider it insufficient?
options: The Act gave Indians full legislative powers but excluded them from the executive — Extremists wanted executive power

The Act increased Council membership and introduced limited nomination through bodies like universities, but maintained British official majorities, kept powers advisory, and introduced no direct elections — achieving little real Indian influence
The Act was never actually implemented — it was passed by Parliament but vetoed by the Viceroy
The Act gave too many powers to Indian princes rather than to elected Indian politicians
questions.explanation: The Indian Councils Act 1892 was the British response to two decades of Moderate petitions — and it was deeply underwhelming. What it did: increased membership in Central and Provincial Legislative Councils; allowed some non-official members to be nominated through bodies like universities, municipalities, and trade associations (a limited indirect form of representation). What it did NOT do: introduce direct elections; give Indians a majority; give Councils real legislative power (they remained advisory/deliberative); give Indians control over the budget. Extremists argued that twenty years of polite petitions had produced a reform so minor as to be insulting — it proved the Moderate method had failed and a more confrontational approach was needed.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the specific limitations of the Indian Councils Act 1892 (no elections, no majority, advisory only) are tested in questions asking why the Moderate era ended

questions.text: Surendranath Banerjee is called the 'Father of Indian Nationalism.' What earned him this title?
options: He was the first Indian to give a speech in the British Parliament and coined the term 'Indian nation'

He founded the Indian Association (1876) — the first organisation with genuinely national ambitions — organized the first National Conferences (1883, 1885), and built political consciousness before the INC existed
He wrote the first Indian constitution and drafted the demands later adopted by the INC
He led the first mass agitation against the British involving hundreds of thousands of peasants
questions.explanation: Surendranath Banerjee (1848-1925) earned the title 'Father of Indian Nationalism' for his pre-INC work: (1) He founded the Indian Association in 1876 — the first political organisation that consciously tried to build a national (not regional) base, recruiting members from across India; (2) He organised two National Conferences (1883 and 1885) that brought delegates from different provinces together to discuss common political concerns; (3) His personal story — unfairly dismissed from the ICS after becoming one of the rare Indians to pass it — made him a symbol of British discrimination and a committed nationalist. His newspaper Bengalee was a platform for political ideas. He built the idea that Indians from different regions shared common political interests before that idea was obvious. The INC (1885) built on the foundation his Indian Association had created.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Surendranath Banerjee is often overshadowed by Gokhale and Naoroji; the Indian Association (1876) as the founding contribution is a specific detail question

questions.text: The age limit for ICS examinations was lowered from 21 to 19 years in 1877. Why was this Moderate-era controversy significant?
options: It was insignificant — very few Indians were attempting the ICS exam at this time in any case

Lowering the age limit specifically disadvantaged Indians who needed extra preparation time for a London-based exam; Moderates fought to raise it and hold the exam in India; it became a symbol of systematic exclusion of Indians from governing their own country
The age limit change allowed British soldiers returning from India to compete for ICS positions, taking posts away from both Indians and British civilians
It was a positive change — younger officers were considered more adaptable to Indian conditions
questions.explanation: The ICS age limit reduction (from 21 to 19 in 1877, under Lord Lytton) was widely seen as a deliberate policy to disadvantage Indians: (1) The exam was held only in London — Indians had to travel overseas, fund the trip, adjust to a foreign environment; (2) Reducing the age limit gave Indians even less preparation time; (3) The subjects tested included European history and classical languages — areas where Indians needed extra study time. The Moderate Congress fought for two demands: hold the exam simultaneously in India AND raise the age limit. This became a concrete, specific symbol of how British India systematically excluded Indians from senior positions in their own government. The demand was only partially met decades later.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the ICS age limit debate is rarely taught in detail; the 21 → 19 reduction and Moderate campaign is a specific detail that appears in questions about Moderate demands and British discrimination

questions.text: Poona Sarvajanik Sabha (1870) was an important precursor to the INC. Who founded it, and why is it significant?
options: Founded by Bal Gangadhar Tilak in 1870; it was the first organisation to demand Swaraj

Founded by M.G. Ranade in 1870 in Pune; it was one of the earliest political organisations working to articulate Indian grievances through public meetings and petitions — a direct predecessor to the INC's constitutional method
Founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale as a predecessor to the Servants of India Society
Founded by Lord Ripon as part of his local self-government reforms — Indians ran it independently from 1882
questions.explanation: The Poona Sarvajanik Sabha (Public Association of Poona) was founded in 1870 by Mahadev Govind Ranade — the same Ranade who led the Prarthana Samaj and was a key Moderate figure. 'Sarvajanik' means public/for all people. It worked to collect and articulate Indian grievances through public meetings, petitions to the government, and surveys of local conditions — the same constitutional method the INC would later use nationally. It was particularly active during the 1876-78 famine, documenting suffering and demanding relief. Ranade also used it to lobby against measures he considered unjust. The Sabha's methods were essentially a prototype for the INC's Moderate approach. Tilak would later become associated with the Sabha, but Ranade founded it.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Poona Sarvajanik Sabha is a precursor organisation rarely taught in detail; the Ranade-Tilak connection (both associated with it at different times) is a specific crossover question

questions.text:
Match the leader with the title or epithet associated with them:
1. Dadabhai Naoroji      A. Lion of Bombay
2. Pherozeshah Mehta     B. Grand Old Man of India
3. Surendranath Banerjee C. Prince of Workers
4. Gopal Krishna Gokhale D. Father of Indian Nationalism
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Dadabhai Naoroji → B. 'Grand Old Man of India' (for his long career, Drain of Wealth theory, and role as elder statesman of nationalism); (2) Pherozeshah Mehta → A. 'Lion of Bombay' (dominant figure in Bombay civic and Congress politics); (3) Surendranath Banerjee → D. 'Father of Indian Nationalism' (founded Indian Association 1876; National Conferences 1883/85; built national political consciousness before INC); (4) Gopal Krishna Gokhale → C. 'Prince of Workers' (for his hardworking, research-based, dedicated approach to public service). This epithet-matching is the single most tested format for Moderate leaders in SSC CGL — all four epiteths appear in virtually every paper covering this topic.
questions.source: Moderate leader epithet matching is the most frequently tested question format for this topic in SSC CGL; all four epiteths (Grand Old Man, Lion of Bombay, Father of Indian Nationalism, Prince of Workers) appear repeatedly

questions.text: The methods used by the Moderate phase of the Indian National Congress (1885-1905) are often summarised as the '3 Ps.' Which of the following correctly identifies these?
options: Publicity, Propaganda, Protest
Petition, Prayer, Protest
Parliament, Press, Protest
Petition, Press, Propaganda
questions.explanation: The Moderate method is summarised as the '3 Ps': Petition (written memorials submitted to the Viceroy, Secretary of State, and British Parliament), Prayer (formal requests and respectful appeals — the term reflects their deferential approach to British authority), and Protest (public meetings, newspaper articles, resolutions — but all peaceful and constitutional, never confrontational or mass-based). The Moderates had absolute faith in British constitutionalism — they believed rational, well-documented cases would be heard and acted upon. The Extremists (from 1905) argued these methods had failed and called for mass agitation, self-help, boycott, and eventually Swaraj.
questions.source: The '3 Ps of Moderate method' (Petition, Prayer, Protest) is one of the most frequently tested characterisation questions for the Moderate Phase in SSC CGL

questions.text: Which of the following 'firsts' related to the Indian National Congress is INCORRECTLY matched?
options: First INC President: W.C. Bonnerjee (1885)
First Muslim INC President: Badruddin Tyabji (1887)
First woman INC President: Sarojini Naidu (1917)
First European INC President: Sir William Wedderburn (1889)
questions.explanation: The incorrectly matched fact is option C. Sarojini Naidu was NOT the first woman INC President in 1917 — Annie Besant was the first woman president of INC, presiding over the 1917 Calcutta session. Sarojini Naidu became the first INDIAN woman president in 1925 (Kanpur session). All other options are correct: W.C. Bonnerjee was indeed the first INC President (1885); Badruddin Tyabji was the first Muslim INC President (1887, Madras session); Sir William Wedderburn was the first European INC President (1889). The Annie Besant vs. Sarojini Naidu distinction is the most frequently tested 'first woman' trap in INC history — both were women presidents but in different years (1917 and 1925).
questions.source: Annie Besant (1917) vs. Sarojini Naidu (1925) as first woman INC President is one of the most frequently tested 'first woman' confusion questions in SSC CGL Modern History
